misconceptionSummary: Students apply the associative law to subtraction, changing the value by moving brackets.

steps.narration: The student moved the brackets as if subtraction were associative. But twenty minus the quantity eight minus three is fifteen, not nine. Moving brackets in a subtraction changes the answer.

visuals.ruleText: Associativity holds for addition only — never apply it across subtraction signs
visuals.subText: a − (b − c) = a − b + c ≠ (a − b) − c

steps.narration: Subtracting a bracket means subtracting everything inside it. When the bracket contains a subtraction, removing the bracket flips the inner sign — minus eight minus three becomes minus eight plus three, not minus eight minus three.
Remove the bracket carefully: subtracting the whole bracket flips every sign inside it. Minus eight minus three becomes minus eight plus three. Then calculate left to right.

visuals.label: Subtraction flips inner signs — associativity does not apply

steps.narration: Fifteen. Always expand brackets in subtraction by flipping every sign inside. The associative law is for addition only.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: treating 20 − (8 − 3) as (20 − 8) − 3 = 9. Subtraction is not associative. Remove the bracket by flipping inner signs: 20 − 8 + 3 = 15.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Never move brackets across a minus sign as if it were a plus. Subtracting a bracket flips all the signs inside it. Associativity only applies to addition.
